data.title: Santos FY Underlying Profit US$508 Million, Down 22%
Santos 1H Profit Falls, Extends Exclusivity Period With XRG Consortium
data.description:
(23:17 GMT) *Santos Says Finalizing Scheme Implementation Agreement With XRG-Led Consortium
(23:17 GMT) *Santos: XRG-Led Consortium Hasn't Found Anything in Due Diligence That Would Lead It to Withdraw Transaction
(23:18 GMT) *Santos: XRG-Led Consortium Requests Extension of Exclusivity Period
(23:18 GMT) *Santos: Agrees to Extend Exclusivity Period for XRG-Led Consortium to Sept 19
(23:27 GMT) Santos 1H Profit Falls, Extends Exclusivity Period With XRG Consortium

By David Winning

SYDNEY--Santos said its half-year profit fell by 31%, as it extended the exclusivity period for a consortium led by Abu Dhabi National Oil Co. unit XRG to complete due diligence and finalize a US$18.72 billion takeover.

Santos said its net profit totaled US$439 million in the six months through June, down from US$636 million a year ago. Underlying profit, which strips out some one-off items, fell by 22% to US$508 million.

Directors of the company declared an interim dividend of 13.4 U.S. cents, up from a payout of 13.0 U.S. cents at the corresponding stage of 2024.

Santos produced 44.1 million barrels of oil equivalent in its fiscal first half, roughly flat on a year earlier. Sales volumes rose by 2% to 47.2 million BOE, although lower energy prices meant half-year revenue fell by 5% to US$2.58 billion. Santos last month lowered the top end of its annual output guidance, citing the impact of flooding in the Cooper Basin.

Monday's result comes as the consortium led by XRG continues to scrutinize Santos's books to determine whether it will proceed with a takeover. On Monday, Santos again extended an exclusivity period for the consortium to firm up a bid, this time to Sept. 19.

The consortium has made an indicative proposal worth 8.89 Australian dollars a share, equivalent to US$5.76, in cash. It had earlier made two approaches worth A$8.00 a share and A$8.60 a share, respectively.

Santos has said it intends to recommend that shareholders accept the deal terms if a binding offer is made by the consortium, which also includes private-equity firm Carlyle.

"On August 24, the XRG Consortium again confirmed it has not found anything in due diligence that would lead it to withdraw its indicative proposal," Santos said in Monday's regulatory filing.

Still, the consortium needs the support of Australia's foreign-investment watchdog and Treasurer Jim Chalmers to complete the deal, which would rank as one of the largest in the energy sector this year. Some analysts have turned more positive about the deal's chances, noting that the consortium could commit to developing natural-gas deposits in New South Wales state that could feed the domestic market and ease concerns about a supply shortfall in future years.

The XRG consortium has a chance to talk up that strategy with regulators and the government as Santos has signaled that it intends to focus on returning more cash to shareholders once its big new projects--Barossa in Australia and Pikka in Alaska--are online.

Santos owns the undeveloped Narrabri natural-gas project in New South Wales state, which has faced repeated setbacks to its development but could be unlocked to bolster domestic supply. Santos earlier this month said it has agreed to a nonbinding deal with France's Engie to supply up to 20 petajoules of natural gas a year from Narrabri over at least a decade.

Write to David Winning at david.winning@wsj.com

Solid New Zealand Retail Sales Shows Economic Pulse
data.description:
By James Glynn

SYDNEY--New Zealand retail sales were stronger than expected by economists in the second quarter, defying the central bank's recent assessment that activity stalled over the three month period.

The total volume of retail sales rose by 0.5% in the second quarter compared with the first quarter, Stats NZ said Monday. Economists had expected a 0.1% increase over the quarter.

Sales growth was seen in most industries. Electronic goods, supermarkets, grocery stores, and pharmaceutical retailing saw the largest increase, according to the data.

Eight of the 15 retail industries surveyed had higher sales volumes in the quarter.

The data suggest that consumers are starting to respond to hefty cuts interest rates since mid-2024, with the Reserve Bank of New Zealand cutting again last week and pointing to the likelihood of more before the year is out.

The official cash rate was lowered to 3.00% from 3.25% on Wednesday, in line with the broad consensus of economists.

The RBNZ said it considered a 50-basis-point reduction and expects to cut further if inflation remains muted.

A year ago the OCR stood at 5.5%, and the central bank has been slashing rates since in response to falling inflation and a sharp rise in unemployment.

New Zealand's economy is still trying to shake off a prolonged downturn, with recent data showing that unemployment continues to rise.

Acting RBNZ Governor Christian Hawkesby said last week the rate cut was required after the economy "stalled" in the second quarter.

-Write to James Glynn at james.glynn@wsj.com

data.title: Regis Resources Target Price Cut 2.2% to A$4.40/Share by Macquarie>RRL.AU
Keurig Dr Pepper reportedly near $18 billion deal to buy coffee company JDE Peet's
data.description:
By Mike Murphy

A pedestrian walks by a Peet's Coffee store in San Francisco in 2024

Keurig Dr Pepper Inc. is closing in on a roughly $18 billion deal to acquire the Dutch coffee company JDE Peet's NV, the Wall Street Journal reported late Sunday.

If the deal goes through, the combined company would split its beverage and coffee units, the Journal reported, effectively unwinding the 2018 deal that merged Dr Pepper and Keurig. Analysts questioned the wisdom of that deal at the time, and while the company's beverage unit has thrived, its coffee segment has struggled.

Neither company immediately responded to requests for comment late Sunday.

Texas-based Keurig Dr Pepper has a market cap of nearly $48 billion, and owns a portfolio of more than 125 brands, including 7-Up, Canada Dry, Snapple, Tully's Coffee and Green Mountain coffee.

Amsterdam-based JDE Peet's has a market cap of around $15 billion, with brands that include Peet's Coffee, Stumptown Coffee Roasters and Maxwell House.

JDE Peet's (NL:JDEP) Dutch-traded shares have surged about 61% year to date. Keurig Dr Pepper stock (KDP) is up about 9% in 2025, compared to the S&P 500's SPX 10% gain.

-Mike Murphy

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Australia's Santos extends exclusivity period for $18.7 billion ADNOC-led offer
data.description: Australian oil and gas producer Santos said on Monday it has agreed to an extension of the exclusivity period for an $18.7 billion takeover bid from a group led by Abu Dhabi National Oil Co to September 19.

data.description:
Santos Ltd :

  • XRG CONSORTIUM INDICATIVE PROPOSAL UPDATE-STO.AX

  • SANTOS HAS AGREED TO AN EXTENSION OF PROCESS DEED TO FRIDAY, 19 SEPTEMBER 2025

  • SANTOS AND XRG CONSORTIUM ARE FINALISING SIA

  • SANTOS NOTES THERE IS NO CERTAINTY THAT A BINDING SIA WILL BE AGREED OR THAT A POTENTIAL TRANSACTION WILL PROCEED.

  • XRG CONSORTIUM AGAIN CONFIRMED IT HAS NOT FOUND ANYTHING IN DUE DILIGENCE THAT WOULD LEAD IT TO WITHDRAW ITS INDICATIVE PROPOSAL
